fiscal_aggregates_2025_26.source: South Africa Budget Review 2026, National Treasury, 25 February 2026
fiscal_aggregates_2025_26.notes: Key macro aggregates from 2026 Budget Review covering 2025/26 fiscal year. Gross debt-to-GDP of 78.9% marks a projected peak — first debt stabilisation in 17 years. Primary surplus of 0.9% of GDP achieved. Tax-to-GDP rises to 25.9% (from 25.1% in 2024/25), reflecting SARS modernisation and improved compliance. Infrastructure MTEF total of R526.3bn focuses on water and roads. SRD grant extended to March 2026 at cost of R35.2bn.

1.revenue_note: Uplift through reduced load-shedding costs (~R204bn/yr per Eskom estimates), logistics efficiency gains on Transnet corridor, and economic-activity multiplier. Does not capture avoided-cost savings to households and firms from ending load-shedding.
key_assumptions: Eskom unbundling and transmission entity established by 2027
Private generation programme achieves 8,500 MW new licensed capacity by 2028
Transnet port productivity recovers to 2017 benchmark levels by 2028
World Bank infrastructure investment multiplier of 1.5× applied to public spend
Blended-finance mechanism mobilises R2.86 in private capital per R1 of public anchor
primary_sources: World Bank South Africa Economic Update 2023 — infrastructure gap estimates
National Treasury Budget Review 2024 — Eskom debt-relief package
CSIR load-shedding economic impact study 2023 (R204bn annual loss estimate)
Transnet Freight Rail 2023/24 Annual Report — productivity benchmarks
Presidential Infrastructure Coordinating Commission (PICC) pipeline 2023

2.revenue_note: Revenue uplift through broadened income-tax and VAT base as informal workers formalise. Employment Tax Incentive (ETI) expansion costs R5bn/yr but is offset by PAYE gains within 3 years of job creation.
key_assumptions: Regulatory reform (Companies Amendment Act, licencing rationalisation) reduces SMME compliance cost by 30%
SEFA/SBSA blended-finance facilities catalyse R90bn in private SMME lending over 5 years
Labour-market flexibility reforms reduce hiring friction in the 10–49 employee band
NDP assumption that SMMEs account for 90% of net new employment holds
Formalisation rate of the informal sector reaches 25% by 2030 (from ~18% baseline)
primary_sources: National Development Plan 2030 — employment targets and SMME projections
OECD SME and Entrepreneurship Outlook 2023 — regulatory burden benchmarks
SEDA Annual Report 2022/23 — SMME development fund disbursements
National Treasury Employment Tax Incentive evaluation 2023
World Bank Doing Business / Business Ready 2024 — SA regulatory environment

3.revenue_note: Revenue uplift is primarily structural: a higher-skilled workforce raises average earnings and therefore PAYE receipts, and reduces expenditure on social grants and remedial programmes. Returns are back-loaded — most impact accrues post-2032.
key_assumptions: TVET college throughput rate improves from 40% to 65% by 2030 with curriculum reform
Early Childhood Development (ECD) subsidised enrolment reaches 85% by 2028
SETA rationalisation releases R8bn/yr for industry-relevant artisan training
University dropout rate falls by 15 percentage points through NSFAS reform
Skills-to-jobs matching efficiency improves via digital labour-market platform
Education quality improvements follow Hanushek-Woessmann human-capital growth model
primary_sources: DHET White Paper for Post-School Education and Training 2013 (updated targets)
National Treasury ECD costing study 2020 — R28bn gap estimate
Hanushek & Woessmann (2012) — human capital and economic growth (SA calibration)
SETA landscape review — National Treasury 2022
NSFAS 2023 Annual Report — throughput and dropout analysis

4.revenue_note: Revenue gains through higher customs receipts (AfCFTA and AGOA export growth), corporate income tax from expanded manufacturing base, and SEZ company tax receipts. The EV transition creates a new R60bn annual export opportunity by 2032 if managed correctly.
key_assumptions: AGOA retained through 2035 (or equivalent EU/AfCFTA arrangements activated)
AfCFTA full implementation delivers 30% intra-African trade growth by 2030 for SA
Automotive Master Plan targets maintained: 1% global market share, 50% localisation
SEZ expansion adds 12 new operational zones with improved utility provision
Green Hydrogen commercialisation achieves first export contracts by 2028
primary_sources: DTIC Automotive Master Plan 2035 — employment and investment targets
AfCFTA Secretariat — SA trade impact modelling 2022
UNCTAD Investment Policy Review: South Africa 2023
AGOA utilisation review — USITC 2023
DTIC Green Hydrogen Commercialisation Strategy 2022

5.revenue_note: Highest return package by fiscal ROI. The SIU and GIZ estimate R100–200bn/yr in procurement irregularities alone. SARS modernisation is projected to close a R50bn/yr compliance gap. Indirect employment gains accrue as investor confidence improves and the regulatory environment becomes predictable.
key_assumptions: SARS technology modernisation (revenue authority digital platform) achieves R50bn/yr additional collections
Procurement reform under revised PPPFA eliminates R30bn/yr in irregular expenditure
Anti-corruption institutions (NPA, SIU, DPCI) receive sustained budget and independence
Digital government platform (GovTech) reduces service-delivery cost by 15%
Consequence management framework leads to measurable reduction in audit findings within 3 years
primary_sources: Special Investigating Unit (SIU) — procurement irregularities consolidated report 2023
GIZ / DPME — State Capacity Diagnostic 2022 (R100–200bn annual loss estimate)
SARS Modernisation Programme business case — National Treasury 2023
Auditor-General SA — Consolidated General Report 2022/23
World Bank Governance Indicators — SA public-sector efficiency scores
